entry.title: AI-Assisted Commodity Work Center: For Smarter Commodity Trading
entry.content:
Commodity trading has never been more complex or more time sensitive. Global energy and agriculture markets move at extraordinary speed, shaped by volatile prices, regulatory shifts, logistics constraints, and constant streams of data. In this environment, traders don’t just need more information—they need intelligent support that helps them act faster and with confidence. 

That’s where AI Assisted Commodity Work Center (ACW) comes in. 

ACW is purpose-built to empower commodity traders with intelligent automation, real-time interaction, and seamless collaboration, all designed to match the pace and complexity of modern trading operations. 

 

Automate What Matters Most 

Traders should spend their time making decisions, not formatting documents or chasing repetitive tasks. 

ACW introduces AI agents that streamline and accelerate document creation directly through BTP to SAP S/4HANA by processing trade summaries written in natural language using Large Language Models. From trade summaries to the creation of draft commodity deals and contracts, ACW automates data capture tasks that traditionally slow traders down. 

By eliminating manual steps and reducing rework: 

  • Uses natural language to capture and process trade summaries from various sources—such as chats, emails, meeting transcripts, and more—so traders don’t need to follow specific trade summary formats 
  • Examples: 
    • Purchase 3 Ships (28,000 barrels 60F / ship) ULSD Diesel from Oil Company, one lifting per month for JFM 2026 (3 total lifts), priced at the Monthly Average Chicago OPIS Index plus $0.05. FOB Chicago Ship. 
    • Purchase Golden Harvest Inc 1000 MT Soybeans Grade A CIF Baton Rouge ZSK2026 Jan - March by ship 
  • Traders reclaim valuable time during peak trading hours 
  • Errors caused by manual data capture are significantly reduced 
  • Operational efficiency improves without disrupting existing systems 

The result? Traders stay focused on strategy and execution, while ACW handles the administrative elements of deal capture.  

 

Capture deals with confidence 

In commodity markets, timing is everything. Delayed decisions can mean missed opportunities, or increased risk. 

ACW delivers real-time AI driven data capture that allows traders to instantly convert trade summaries into actionable documents. Instead of switching between systems or waiting on downstream processes, traders can move seamlessly from insight to execution. 

With ACW: 

  • Critical information is surfaced and validated when it’s needed most 
  • Trade-related data is contextualized, not just displayed 
  • Data capture becomes faster, more accurate, and more consistent across multiple deal types 

Progress Bar

With error indicator Indicating warnings to be reviewed by the user Ready to create the document 

This means that traders can replace their traditional handwritten deal sheets/trade books by writing the same trade summary into ACW and have the trade created in the system. 

 

Scale with intelligence 

As organizations grow, so do their trading teams. But scaling expertise has traditionally been difficult; new traders face steep learning curves, complex systems, and fragmented workflows. ACW changes that dynamic. 

With an intuitive, trader-centric user interface, ACW enables rapid deal capture for every trader, regardless of experience level. The platform is designed to feel natural, reducing onboarding time and helping teams become productive faster. 

This means: 

  • Faster ramp-up for new traders 
  • More consistent execution across teams 
  • Institutional knowledge embedded into everyday workflows 

Message Bar

Easy-to-understand warning and error messages helping traders to identify what needs to be done. 

 

Built for the reality of commodity markets 

ACW is not a generic AI tool, it’s designed specifically for commodity trading organizations, where precision, speed, and compliance matter. By integrating directly into existing enterprise environments and aligning with real trader workflows, ACW enhances how teams work without forcing them to work differently. 

 

Conclusion - The Future of Trading Starts Here 

ACW represents a new standard for commodity trading operations—one where AI works alongside traders, not instead of them. By automating what matters most, delivering real-time AI driven data capture, and scaling intelligence across teams, ACW enables traders to operate with greater speed, clarity, and confidence. 

In markets where every decision counts, ACW is the AI Work Center that keeps traders ahead. 

SAP Help Documentation 

  • Admin Guide: Overview | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: KI-Kompetenzentwicklung und Weiterbildung - Praxiserfahrungen und bewährte Ansätze
entry.content:
In der SAP Training und Change Management Community Session ging es im Februar um KI Kompetenzentwicklung. Diesesmal habe ich nicht moderiert, sondern selbst einen Impuls gegeben. 

Als Intro habe ich unterschiedliche Befragungsergebnisse vorgestellt. Eine Studie von Stifterverband und McKinsey mit rund 1000 Firmen zeigt beispielsweise, dass bei fast 80 Prozent befragter deutscher Firmen grundlegende KI-Kenntnisse fehlen.

Danach stellte ich unser SAP AI Skill-Self Assessment vor. Die Auswertung von ca. 130 Befragten zeigt: Motivation ist am höchsten, dann kommt Awareness. Beim Wissen zeigen sich Lücken, und die Implementierungskompetenz fällt am geringsten aus. Die hohe Motivation bildet eine gute Basis für die Weiterentwicklung von Awareness, Wissen und vor allem der praktischen Anwendungskompetenz. Details zur Auswertung findet Ihr hier. 

Sieben Tipps zur KI-Kompetenzentwicklung

Tipp 1: KI-Grundlagen für alle

Ich sehe grundlegende KI-Kenntnisse als essenziell für alle – vom Schüler bis zum SAP-Entwickler. In der SAP Community haben wir mit Kollegen eine kuratierte Liste von SAP AI Trainings erstellt, die in etwa einem Tag absolvierbar ist. Die Grundlagenkurse decken Joule Agents, Business AI allgemein und Responsible AI Themen wie Ethik ab. 

Formelles Lernen durch E-Learning macht für Grundlagen absolut Sinn. Neben SAP-Angeboten empfehle ich Microsoft Akademien, DeepLearning.AI, Open HPI mit deutschsprachigen Onlinekursen oder den KI Campus. Viele Angebote sind kostenfrei. Bereits bei den Grundlagen ist der Austausch in Communities wichtig sowie das Ausprobieren.

Tipp 2: Selbsteinschätzung durchführen - Wo stehe ich?

Ich rate immer zu einer Standortbestimmung für sich selbst und das Team. Ein Self-Assessment für sich oder sein Team bietet eine einfache Selbsteinschätzung. Dahinter können verschiedene Kompetenzmodelle stehen.

Das Modell von Stifterverband und McKinsey unterscheidet zwischen Grundwissen, praktischer generativer KI-Kompetenz wie Prompting, Inhalteerstellung und Prozessautomatisierung sowie analytischen Kompetenzen. Die Modell- und Toolauswahl bleibt wichtig – es braucht ein Gefühl dafür, welches Tool für welche Herausforderung passt, ob Gemini, Claude oder SAP-Lösungen. Datengetriebenes Entscheiden und datenbasiertes Handeln gehören ebenso dazu wie die kritische ethische Einordnung von KI.

Das AI Competency Framework von Next Education von Professor Ehlers ist allgemeiner aufgestellt mit Future Skills für eine durch KI geprägte Lebenswelt. Es umfasst das Arbeiten und Gestalten mit und für KI als Umsetzungskompetenz, persönliche Fähigkeiten wie kreative Problemlösung sowie die Gestaltung des sozialen Umfelds mit ethischer und Kooperationskompetenz.

Mein pragmatisches Modell gliedert sich in technische, analytische, soziale, ethische und Veränderungskompetenz. Es folgt dem Schema Skills, Knowledge und Attitudes – man muss etwas wissen, etwas können, und bei manchen Themen wie Ethik geht es auch um Einstellungen und Haltung.

In der Session führten wir eine Live-Selbsteinschätzung per Mentimeter durch. Die Teilnehmenden ordneten sich bei verschiedenen SAP-KI-Themen zwischen Awareness und Practice ein und wir sahen, dass sich die meisten im Bereich Awareness verorten.

Tipp 3: Rollenbasierte Lernpläne für KI

Nach der Selbsteinschätzung sollte man sich die Frage stellen: Wo will ich hin? Ein Lernplan sollte nach Rolle und Qualifikation ausgerichtet sein. Die Anforderungen für Entwickler beispielsweise unterscheiden sich von denen für Beraterinnen.

In der SAP Community habe ich einen Blog mit einer Vorauswahl und Empfehlung für KI Lerninhalte erstellt. Es gibt hier rollenbasierte Angebote für Business User, Admins, Berater und technische KI-Lernangebote. Ein neuer Trainer-geführter Kurs "Applying Business AI Solutions in Finance" steht als Virtual Training Lab oder im Klassenzimmer zur Verfügung.

Auch Live-Sessions werden regelmäßig aktualisiert, derzeit gibt es 186 Sessions rund um KI-Themen. Übungssysteme zum Ausprobieren, Zertifizierungen wie für den GenAI Developer oder Positioning SAP Business AI für Presales und Beratung ergänzen das Angebot.

Tipp 4: Lernziele setzen und Fortschritt dokumentieren

Verbunden mit dem Punkt davor ist es hilfreich sich konkrete Lernziele zu setzen. Nicht nur "ich will KI lernen", sondern beispielsweise "bis Ende März die drei Joule Grundkurse absolvieren, Projekte umsetzen und Blogbeiträge lesen".

Das Reflektieren und Teilen der Lernerfahrung unterstützt das Behalten und die Verankerung. Dies kann über Blogs, unsere LinkedIn Gruppe, die SAP Community Gruppe hier, Lerntagebücher oder Gespräche mit Kollegen erfolgen. Manche nutzen feste Termine, andere arbeiten selbstgesteuert.

Im Chat sammelten wir kurz Ergebnisse zu den letzten Lernerfahrungen. Die Teilnehmenden teilten: Nutzung von Microsoft Copilot, Synthesia für Videocontent und Trainingsinhalten, KI als Sparringpartner bei der Konzepterstellung, AI Agents, AI Content Production, Kulturautobiografie via AI Video und Podcasts.

Tipp 5: Mit und von anderen lernen

Ich bin seit Jahren Fan von Peer Learning über Formate wie Barcamps, Workshops, Lerngruppen, Promptatons, Communities und Live-Sessions. Der Austausch mit anderen unterstützt die Kompetenzentwicklung durch Reflexion und unterschiedliche Perspektiven.

Promptatons als Lightweight-Hackathon

Promptatons sind ein niedrigschwelliges Format, eine Art Lightweight-Hackathon von zwei Stunden bis zu einem Tag, hybrid, virtuell oder vor Ort. Bei SAP finden Beispielsweise Sessions mit über 100 Personen virtuell statt.

Das Format besteht aus minimalem Input zu gutem Prompting und Tools die man nutzen kann, dann folgen Challenges aus der Lebensrealität der Teilnehmenden. Mindestens eine Stunde, besser eineinhalb Stunden arbeiten Kleingruppen von drei bis vier Personen hands-on, unterstützt durch Coaches. Am Ende erfolgt ein Report-back zu Lessons Learned und Lösungswegen.

Die Challenges sollten aus der Praxis stammen und in Schwierigkeitsstufen verfügbar sein. Level 1 wäre beispielsweise das Designen von Personas, Zusammenfassen von Videos oder Schreiben von Blogs. Schwierigere Herausforderungen wären  Agenten oder GPTs bauen, etwa einen Lernbegleiter. Stretch-Goals fordern zusätzlich heraus.

Hier ein Beispiel: Innovative Idea generation (Level 2)

Create a prompt to brainstorm innovative ideas for a new product or service. Simulate a brainstorming session that generate ideas based on customer needs, industry trends etc. The final output should include a list of ideas with their potential impact.
Stretch Goal: Rank these ideas based on feasibility, viability, and desirability.
Tools to use: xy tool accessible for the group (or bring your own AI Tool). 
Wichtig ist ein digitales Whiteboard oder Brown Paper vor Ort, Selbstorganisation der Gruppen und Coaches mit Tool- und Prompting-Expertise. Die Reflexion des Lösungswegs sind hilfreich. Viele wünschen sich längeren Zugriff auf die erarbeiteten Prompts. 
Firmen wie Continental, Telekom, DM setzen Promptatons ein, um konkrete Anwendungsfälle aus zu probieren.

Communities und Lerngruppen

In der SAP Community gibt es die u.a. die AIDeveloper Community Gruppe, die AI Learning Group oder eben die Gruppe hier zu Lernen, Change & Transformation. Dort finden sich immer Blogs und Diskussionen im Forum. Man kann dabei durch eigene Fragen und durch das Beantworten von Fragen anderer lernen. Zusätzlich gibt es unterschiedliche Events zum Austausch.

Die neue AI Learning Group teilt vor allem neue Kurse, kuratiert von einem SAP Experten. Marc Green, ein erfahrener SAP-Mastertrainer, moderiert ebenfalls eine Gruppe und erklärt Themen in kurzen Videos mit viel Erfahrungswissen.

Lernleitfäden für kleine Lerngruppen sind eine weitere Möglichkeit. Die Learning Circle Experience zu Change Management funktionierte in Kleingruppen mit einem Leitfaden voller Impulse und Übungen. Der LernOS Lernleitfaden für generative KI kann von Teams oder Lerngruppen genutzt werden. Die Erfahrungen zeigen, dass manche auch die Termine komprimieren. So kann man sich statt drei Monate wöchentlich beispielsweise drei bis vier mal komprimiert treffen. Die Leitfäden verbinden das Lernen von und miteinander, sind relativ kostenneutral und haben das Element der sozialen Unterstützung und des Austausches. 

Tipp 6: Durch eigene Erfahrung und Handeln lernen

KI versteht man nur, indem man es nutzt. Bei einer breiten und mächtigen Technologie muss jeder kontextualisieren und prüfen, wo KI bei den eigenen Aufgaben hilft, wo man kreativer oder effektiver wird. Experimentierräume schaffen und erkunden ist wichtig. So kann man sich Lernprojekten vornehmen, um ein konkretes Problem mit KI zu lösen.

SAP AppHaus Methoden

Die SAP AppHaus Methoden bieten verschiedene Tools rund um KI. Der AI Exploration Workshop prüft Use Cases nach dem Dreiklang aus Design Thinking: Feasibility, Viability und Desirability. Es gibt einen Leitfaden für einen Tag oder eine Mini-Version für zwei Stunden. Weitere Vorlagen zur Agent Discovery und Agent Design führen einen Schritt weiter.

Diese Workshops können strategisch top-down umgesetzt werden. Ich empfehle zudem, dass jedes Team einen Business AI Exploration Workshop durchführt und gemeinsam AI Anwendungsfälle exploriert. 

Anbei die Chat-Ergebnisse zu Lieblings-Use-Cases der Teilnehmenden: ChatGPT für Programmierung, Zusammenfassung von To-Dos aus Meetings, Workshopvorbereitung, Konzepte, kritisches Feedback zu Konzepten, Copilot für Einsatzpläne von Azubis, Agenten für Onboarding-Fragen, Perplexity für Recherche, Textarbeit wie Vorbereitung, Erstellung, Strukturierung und Personalisierung, Daily Digest zur Strukturierung von Outlook. Ein Beispiel aus meiner Praxis: Bei Podcasts unterstützt KI bei Skriptvorbereitung, Recherche, Audio-to-Text-Transkription, Zusammenfassung als Newsletter, Covergestaltung und Bildbearbeitung.

Phasen beim AI Rollout war darauf eine Frage im Chat. Ich empfehle, Grundlagen und Anwendung parallel zu verbinden. Einfache Use Cases wie E-Mail schreiben, Meetings zusammenfassen oder Recherche kann man früh integrieren, statt eine große Schulungswelle vorweg zu machen ohne konkrete Anwendung. Bei KI Grundlagenschulungen ohne jegliche Anwendung wird sonst nur das Meiste vergessen. 

Tipp 7: Regelmäßig KI Kenntnisse updaten

Lernroutinen des regelmässigen Updaten sind notwendig, da KI sich schnell entwickelt. Der SAP AI-Newsletter, unsere Podcasts auf Apple, Spotify liefern solche regelmäßigen Updates. Veranstaltungen von SAP und Partnern, die TechEd für Entwickler und technisch Interessierte mit kostenfreien Inhalten sind weitere Quellen.

Ein Meta-Ansatz ist die Erstellung von AI News Agenten, etwa mit ChatGPT oder Perplexity, die zu speziellen Themen recherchieren und täglich oder wöchentlich ein Digest mit Links und kritischer Einordnung liefern.

Mein Tipp: KI immer einen Platz am Tisch geben. Bei jedem neuen Thema sollte man überlegen, wie KI helfen kann, auch multimodal oder im Dialog.

Chat-Ergebnisse zu Update-Quellen waren: The Neuron Newsletter, One Useful Thing | Ethan Mollick, heise KI Update, Doppelgänger TechTalk, SAP Learning Newsletter, KI-Podcast der ARD, Carrie Speaker auf TikTok, LinkedIn mit Experten wie Dennis Hassabis von DeepMind, Snip KI Newsletter, Corporate Learning Camp, Mastodon, YouTube-Kanäle wie Matt Wolf.

Ergänzenden SAP-Angebote und Formate

SAP bietet Kunden Innovation Days für Business AI, Inspiration Sessions als zweitägige Workshops, Maturity Assessments durch das Advisory Team (früher Business Transformation Berater)  zur Standortbestimmung auf der KI-Reise (Kontakt gerne über mich). 

AI Exploration Workshops vom SAP Apphaus und die Tool-Aktivierungsmission für SuccessFactors oder Online-Formate mit Breakout-Sessions sind verfügbar.

Die Agent Days in Berlin die z.B. Ende Februar laufen umfassen zwei Tage mit Inspiration und Breakout-Sessions nach Industrie. Das Thema Agents bietet hohes Potenzial für Automatisierung, gerade mit Joule Studio im SAP-Kontext.

Hackathons am 23.-27. März 2026 in München, Berlin, Zürich dauern eine Woche, erfordern Bewerbung mit Use Cases und bieten enge Betreuung. Dieses Hands-on Format ist aufwendig, aber durch die Konkretheit auch ein sehr starkes Lernformat.

Resüme, Diskussion und weitere Hinweise

Ein Teilnehmer merkte an, dass Context Engineering zwischen Content und Prompting als Kompetenz wichtig ist. Das ist wohl wahr: die Bedeutung von Kontext bei KI, z.B. mit Nutzung von RAG und eigener firmenrelevantem Daten ist absolut wichtig. Kompetenzmodelle sind vereinfachte Abbildungen der Realität und sollten flexibel angepasst werden.

Zusammenfassend ist für KI Kompetenz folgendes wichtig: Grundlagen verstehen, Selbsteinschätzung vornehmen, rollenbasierte Lernpläne erstellen, Ziele setzen und dokumentieren, Peer Learning nutzen, durch Handeln lernen und regelmäßig updaten.

Wir werden diese Themen weiter in der Community beleuchten. Weitere Kaffeeecken zu den Themen S/4 Transformationen, Change Readiness und Business Transformation Formaten sind geplant - ihr findet die Anmeldung wie immer auf der Webseite zur SAP Change & Training Kaffee-Ecke.

entry.title: An AI Tool That Turns SuccessFactors $metadata Into a Clean Excel Data Dictionary in 30 Secs
entry.content:
If you've ever opened a SuccessFactors $metadata XML file in a browser and felt your soul leave your body, this post is for you.

Whether it's an Employee Central rollout or a complex payroll integration there's one task that shows up on every single project and nobody enjoys doing it: documenting the data model.

Every implementation needs a data dictionary. Every integration needs a field mapping sheet. Every security audit asks "what fields exist, what's required, who can edit what?" And the answer to all of these lives in one place: the OData $metadata endpoint. A single XML file that is, on average, 50,000 lines of raw schema definition that no human should ever have to read manually.

So I built a tool to fix that. It uses Claude AI to parse any SuccessFactors $metadata XML file and transform it into professional, color-coded Excel workbooks, Word documents, CSV exports, and even instance comparison reports — in about 30 seconds.

Let me show you how it works.

The Problem: 50,000 Lines of This

Here's what you see when you open https://your-instance.successfactors.com/odata/v2/$metadata in a browser:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<edmx:Edmx Version="1.0" xmlns:edmx="http://schemas.microsoft.com/ado/2007/06/edmx">
  <edmx:DataServices m:DataServiceVersion="2.0">
    <Schema Namespace="SFOData" xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/ado/2008/09/edm">
      <EntityType Name="EmpJob" sap:label="Job Information">
        <Key>
          <PropertyRef Name="seqNumber"/>
          <PropertyRef Name="startDate"/>
          <PropertyRef Name="userId"/>
        </Key>
        <Property Name="seqNumber" Type="Edm.Int64" Nullable="false"
          sap:label="Sequence Number" sap:creatable="false" sap:updatable="false"
          sap:filterable="true" sap:sortable="true"/>
        <Property Name="startDate" Type="Edm.DateTime" Nullable="false"
          sap:label="Start Date" sap:creatable="true" sap:updatable="true"
          sap:filterable="true" sap:sortable="true"/>
        <Property Name="userId" Type="Edm.String" MaxLength="100" Nullable="false"
          sap:label="User ID" sap:creatable="true" sap:updatable="false"
          sap:filterable="true" sap:sortable="true"/>
        <Property Name="company" Type="Edm.String" MaxLength="128"
          sap:label="Company" sap:creatable="true" sap:updatable="true"
          sap:filterable="true"/>
        <Property Name="costCenter" Type="Edm.String" MaxLength="128"
          sap:label="Cost Center" sap:creatable="true" sap:updatable="true"/>
        <Property Name="department" Type="Edm.String" MaxLength="128"
          sap:label="Department" sap:creatable="true" sap:updatable="true"/>
        <Property Name="cust_workShiftCode" Type="Edm.String" MaxLength="255"
          sap:label="Work Shift" sap:creatable="true" sap:updatable="true"
          sap:filterable="true"/>
        <!-- ... 80+ more fields ... -->
        <NavigationProperty Name="companyNav" Relationship="SFOData.EmpJob_company"
          FromRole="EmpJob" ToRole="FOCompany"/>
        <NavigationProperty Name="departmentNav" Relationship="SFOData.EmpJob_department"
          FromRole="EmpJob" ToRole="FODepartment"/>
        <!-- ... 30+ more navigation properties ... -->
      </EntityType>
      <!-- ... 500+ more entity types ... -->
    </Schema>
  </edmx:DataServices>
</edmx:Edmx>

Now multiply this by 500+ entities. That's your $metadata file. Finding "which fields on EmpJob are required and creatable via API" means scrolling through thousands of lines, cross-referencing Nullable="false" with sap:creatable="true", and manually building a spreadsheet.

I've done this. You've done this. We've all done this. And it takes hours.

The Solution: Upload, Ask, Done

Here's the new workflow. It takes three steps.

Step 1: Download Your $metadata

Navigate to your instance's metadata endpoint and save the XML:

 
 
https://your-instance.successfactors.com/odata/v2/$metadata

Save as metadata.xml. This file is typically 5–15 MB depending on your instance configuration.

Step 2: Upload to Claude and Ask

Open Claude (claude.ai or Claude Code) and upload the file. Then just ask for what you need in plain English:

"Parse this SuccessFactors metadata and create an Excel data dictionary with a summary sheet, individual sheets for each Employee Central entity, and a master list of all fields."

Step 3: Get Your Data Dictionary

In about 30 seconds, Claude generates a professional multi-sheet Excel workbook. Here's what each sheet looks like:

What You Get: The Excel Output

Summary Sheet

The first sheet gives you a bird's-eye view of your entire instance:

📊Your SF Data Dictionary is Complete!

Here's what I created for you:

Workbook Contents:

  1. Legend (Quick Reference)
    • Color coding guide
    • Column definitions and what they mean
    • Helps you understand the data at a glance
  2. Summary (Executive Overview)
    • 726 total entities
    • 13,469 total fields across all entities
    • 3,965 associations (relationships between entities)
    • 82 complex types
    • Breakdown by category (Employee Central, Position Management, Compensation, etc.)
  3. All Fields (Master Data Dictionary)
    • Every single field from every entity
    • Sortable and filterable
    • Includes: field name, type, required status, max length, labels, creatable/updatable flags
    • Color-coded for quick identification:
      • Yellow = Key fields (primary identifiers)
      • Light Red = Required fields (must have a value)
      • Light Blue = Custom fields (created via MDF)
  4. Entity-Specific Sheets (Top 30 Entities)
    • Detailed breakdown for the most important entities
    • Includes navigation properties
    • Ready for deep-dive analysis
  5. Navigation Properties (3,529 Relationships)
    • Shows how entities connect to each other
    • From/To entity mappings
    • Essential for understanding data flow and integration points

Use This For:

  • 📋Data Mapping — Connect SF fields to your payroll, ATS, or other systems
  • 🔍Field Discovery — Quickly find what fields exist and what they do
  • 📖Compliance & Documentation — Audit-ready reference for your instance
  • 🔗Integration Planning — Understand relationships between entities
  • 👥Team Communication — Non-technical stakeholders can understand the schema

You can now download, share, and use this in Excel with full filtering, sorting, and pivot table capabilities!

Real-World Use Cases

Let me walk through five scenarios I've personally used this tool on.

Use Case 1: New Implementation Data Dictionary

The ask: "We need a complete data dictionary for our Employee Central go-live documentation."

The prompt:

"Parse this metadata and create an Excel workbook. I need a summary sheet with all entities grouped by SF module, and detailed field sheets for: User, EmpEmployment, EmpJob, EmpCompensation, PerPersonal, PerEmail, PerPhone, PerNationalId, and PerAddressDEFLT. Also include a navigation properties sheet."

Time to deliver: 30 seconds instead of 2–3 days of manual work.

What I used to do: Open the metadata XML in Notepad++, Ctrl+F for each entity, manually copy field names into Excel, cross-reference attributes one by one. For a typical EC implementation with 10 core entities, this was a solid 2-day task.

Use Case 2: Integration Field Mapping

The ask: "Map all creatable fields on EmpJob to our payroll system — we need field name, label, type, and max length."

The prompt:

"From this metadata, create a CSV with all fields on EmpJob where sap:creatable is true. Include columns: Field Name, Label, Data Type, Max Length, Required. Sort by required fields first."

The result: A clean CSV file that the integration team can immediately import into their mapping tool. No manual parsing needed.

Use Case 3: Dev vs. Prod Comparison

This one is my favorite. After a series of transports, the client wanted to verify that their Production instance had all the same custom fields as Development.

The prompt:

"I've uploaded two metadata files — metadata_dev.xml and metadata_prod.xml. Compare them and show me: entities that exist in one but not the other, and custom fields (cust_) that differ between instances."*

The output: A comparison report showing:

  • 3 custom MDF objects existed in DEV but hadn't been transported to PROD
  • 7 custom fields on EmpJob were in PROD but missing from DEV (someone configured directly in Prod — a compliance issue!)
  • 2 fields had different Nullable settings between environments

This kind of configuration drift is a real problem in SF implementations, and this tool catches it instantly.

Use Case 4: Security Audit — "What Can This API User Access?"

The ask: "For our security review, we need to document every field that is filterable and sortable across all entities — these represent potential data extraction vectors."

The prompt:

"Parse this metadata and create an Excel sheet showing all fields across all entities where sap:filterable = true. Group by entity, and add a column showing whether the field contains PII (look for fields with labels containing 'name', 'email', 'phone', 'address', 'national', 'birth', 'gender', 'salary')."

This delivers a ready-made appendix for your security audit documentation.

Use Case 5: Quick Lookup During a Call

Not every use case needs a full export. Sometimes you're on a call with a client and they ask: "Is the businessUnit field on EmpJob required? Can we update it via API?"

The prompt:

"From this metadata, tell me about the businessUnit field on EmpJob — is it required, creatable, updatable, and what's the max length?"

Claude's response:

The businessUnit field on EmpJob is:

  • Type: Text (Edm.String), Max Length 128
  • Required: No (Nullable = true)
  • Creatable: Yes
  • Updatable: Yes
  • Filterable: Yes
  • Label: "Business Unit"

Answer delivered in 5 seconds while you're still on the call.

How to Get Started

Option 1: Use It Right Now in Claude.ai

  1. Go to claude.ai,
  2. Follow instructions provided on GitHub.
  3. Upload your $metadata XML file (download from your instance)
  4. Type your request: "Parse this SuccessFactors metadata into an Excel data dictionary"
  5. Download the generated Excel file

That's it. No installation, no configuration, no code.

Option 2: Install as a Claude Code Plugin

If you use Claude Code (Anthropic's terminal-based coding assistant), you can install this as a permanent plugin:

 

bash
/plugin install shaktibarath/sf-metadata-parser

Once installed, Claude automatically recognizes when you're working with SF metadata and applies the skill.

Option 3: Use via the Claude API

For automated pipelines (e.g., nightly metadata snapshots), you can call the skill programmatically via the Claude API with the Skills endpoint.

How to Download Your $metadata

If you haven't done this before, here's where to find it:

  1. URL format: https://<api-server>/odata/v2/$metadata
  2. Authentication: Use your SF admin credentials (Basic Auth) or an API user
  3. Example API servers by data center:
    • DC2: api2.successfactors.eu
    • DC4: api4.successfactors.com
    • DC8: api8.successfactors.com
    • DC10: api10.successfactors.com
    • DC12: api12.successfactors.com
    • DC55: api55.successfactors.eu
  4. Save as: Right-click → Save As → metadata.xml

Or use the browser developer tools (Network tab) if your instance requires SSO.

For a filtered metadata (specific entities only), append ?$filter= but note that the full metadata is more useful for comprehensive documentation.

What's Next

This tool is open-source on GitHub and I'm actively developing it. If you're an SF consultant, admin, or integration developer — I'd love your feedback. What outputs would save you the most time? What's missing?

Try It Now

The tool is available today:

  • Claude.ai — Upload your metadata XML and start asking questions
  • GitHub — https://github.com/shaktibarath/sf-metadata-parser
  • Claude Code — /plugin install shaktibarath/sf-metadata-parser

If it saves you even one afternoon of manual XML parsing, it was worth building.

Have questions or feedback? Find me on LinkedIn or open an issue on GitHub.

entry.title: LangGraph Checkpoint Saver for SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

LangGraph is becoming a popular framework for building AI agents that need to maintain state across interactions. If you're running these agents on SAP BTP with HANA Cloud as your database, you'll quickly find that LangGraph's built-in persistence options don't include HANA. This post introduces langgraph-checkpoint-hana, an open-source package I built to fill that gap. It implements the full LangGraph checkpointer interface for HANA Cloud, so your agent state lives in the same database as your business data.

What's a checkpointer

LangGraph is a framework for building stateful AI agents. A checkpointer saves the agent's state at each step of execution. This enables:

  • Persistent memory across sessions (the agent remembers previous interactions)
  • Fault recovery (if the process crashes, it resumes from the last saved state instead of starting over)
  • Time travel (you can inspect or roll back to any previous state)

LangGraph ships with checkpointers for PostgreSQL, SQLite, and InMemory. The community has built others for Redis, DynamoDB, Snowflake. None for HANA.

Why HANA

If you're already using HANA Vector Store for RAG (via langchain-hanadb), adding a separate database just for checkpointing adds unnecessary complexity. Vectors and agent state in the same database, same instance, same credentials.

On BTP specifically, HANA Cloud is already there. Your Kyma or CF app already has a HANA binding. Using it for checkpointing means no additional service instances.

Installation

pip install langgraph-checkpoint-hana

Requires hdbcli (installed automatically) and Python 3.10+.

Usage

Three ways to create the checkpointer:

From connection parameters

from langgraph_checkpoint_hana import HANASaver

with HANASaver.from_conn_info(
    address="your-instance.hanacloud.ondemand.com",
    port=443,
    user="DBADMIN",
    password="your-password",
) as checkpointer:
    graph = workflow.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer)
    result = graph.invoke(inputs, {"configurable": {"thread_id": "t1"}})

From environment variables

Useful in containers on Kyma/CF where credentials come from service bindings:

import os
os.environ["HANA_HOST"] = "your-instance.hanacloud.ondemand.com"
os.environ["HANA_PORT"] = "443"
os.environ["HANA_USER"] = "DBADMIN"
os.environ["HANA_PASSWORD"] = "your-password"

from langgraph_checkpoint_hana import HANASaver
checkpointer = HANASaver.from_env()
checkpointer.setup()

From an existing hdbcli connection

If your application already manages its own HANA connections:

from hdbcli import dbapi
from langgraph_checkpoint_hana import HANASaver

conn = dbapi.connect(address="...", port=443, user="...", password="...")
checkpointer = HANASaver(conn=conn)
checkpointer.setup()

What it creates

Calling setup() creates two tables if they don't exist:

  • LANGGRAPH_CHECKPOINTS -- stores graph state snapshots
  • LANGGRAPH_CHECKPOINT_WRITES -- stores pending writes for fault recovery

Both keyed by (thread_id, checkpoint_ns, checkpoint_id). Uses NCLOB columns for serialized data. Table existence is checked via SYS.TABLES, not try/except.

Full example with a LangGraph agent

from langgraph_checkpoint_hana import HANASaver
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, MessagesState, START, END

def chatbot(state: MessagesState) -> MessagesState:
    # your LLM call here
    return {"messages": [response]}

with HANASaver.from_env() as checkpointer:
    workflow = StateGraph(MessagesState)
    workflow.add_node("chatbot", chatbot)
    workflow.add_edge(START, "chatbot")
    workflow.add_edge("chatbot", END)

    graph = workflow.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer)

    config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": "session-42"}}

    # First interaction
    graph.invoke({"messages": [("human", "What's our AP aging?")]}, config)

    # Second interaction - agent remembers the first one
    graph.invoke({"messages": [("human", "Break it down by vendor")]}, config)

    # Check state
    state = graph.get_state(config)

Thread cleanup

# Delete all state for a thread
checkpointer.delete_thread("session-42")

Useful for GDPR compliance, cleaning up test data, or recovering from corrupted state (orphaned tool calls that cause downstream errors -- if you've hit this in production you know what I mean).

Technical notes

  • Writes use HANA's UPSERT ... WITH PRIMARY KEY for atomic insert-or-update
  • Serialization uses LangGraph's JsonPlusSerializer (same as the official checkpointers)
  • Async methods delegate to sync because hdbcli is a synchronous C-extension driver. For high-concurrency async deployments, wrap with asyncio.to_thread()
  • Tested with LangGraph 0.2.x/0.3.x, HANA Cloud 2024.x/2025.x, Python 3.10-3.12

Conclusion

If you're building LangGraph agents on BTP, this package lets you keep agent state in the same HANA Cloud instance where your business data already lives. No extra databases, no extra infrastructure. Install it with pip install langgraph-checkpoint-hana and try it out.

The package is MIT licensed and I welcome contributions. If you run into issues or have suggestions, open an issue on the GitHub repo.

  • PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/langgraph-checkpoint-hana/
  • GitHub: https://github.com/stubborncoder/langgraph-checkpoint-hana

entry.title: UI5con Bengaluru 2026 - Community, Code, and the Future of SAP UI Development
entry.content:
UI5con Bengaluru 2026 brought together 400+ developers, architects, consultants, and students from across India for a full day of learning, hands-on exploration, and community connection. Held at the SAP Labs campus in Devanahalli as part of SAP Inside Track Bengaluru 2026, the event marked a historic double first — the first time UI5con has taken place in India in seven years, and the very first SAP Inside Track and UI5con held jointly at this location. The event covered everything from the cutting-edge world of AI-assisted development to the fundamentals of accessibility and enterprise UX — all through the lens of SAPUI5 and SAP Fiori.

This was more than a conference; it was a homecoming. With UI5con returning to India after seven years and joining forces with SAP Inside Track for the very first time, the energy in the room was something special — a community reunited and ready to build.

"It's my first SAP Inside Track session as an ABAP Fiori developer. I had a great session attending UI5con. Learned some amazing things during the session."
Participant, Deloitte USI

Kickoff: Agentic Coding with UI5 — From Idea to Inclusive App

Stefan Beck, Peter Muessig, and Rajashree Raghavendra opened the day with a keynote that was equal parts history lesson and vision statement — reminding the audience that UI5con Bengaluru began in 2018, grew to 400+ attendees, 28 sessions, and 41 speakers in 2019, and is now back after a COVID-forced pause of seven years. They traced SAPUI5’s journey from its 2012 enterprise foundations to its current form: TypeScript, Web Components, CI/CD, and a 90% accessibility score in the Web Almanac 2025. Two strategic bets anchored the roadmap: UI5 Web Components (framework-free, standards-based) and Legacy-Free OpenUI5/SAPUI5 (deprecated code removed, async by default).

The AI portion of the keynote charted the evolution from basic code completion in 2020 to the agentic coding era of today and introduced the UI5 MCP Server and Fiori MCP Server as the practical bridges between AI tools and SAP development best practices. A live demo closed the session — an inclusive app built from scratch using Fiori elements Building Blocks and Freestyle code, guided by agentic AI in real time. The vision set the tone for everything that followed: "UI5 in 2026 is where AI acceleration, inclusive design, and enterprise trust meet."

AI Takes Centre Stage

If there was one theme that ran through every session at UI5con 2026, it was AI — and not just as a buzzword. The hands-on session on the MCP Server for Fiori, led by Vinay Hospete, Ashish Anand, and Maheeth Reddy, introduced attendees to the Model Context Protocol — the emerging standard that connects AI coding agents to domain-specific tools and best practices. The demo pod on Vibe Coding with SAP UI5 brought this to life, showing how natural language prompts combined with Cline and SAP AI Core enable governed, auditable UI5 app generation — where AI accelerates the work, but the developer stays in control.

"Vibe coding: Using the MCP server, the code can be generated quickly and can be adapted for the demo & quick mockup."
Participant, Nokia

Deep Dives: Going Beyond the Basics

The deep dive track gave attendees space to go further. Merlin Beutlberger's UI5 in the World of AI showcased the UI5 MCP Server's 10 tools — from app creation and API reference lookups to Linter runs and TypeScript migration guides — with a live demo of an AI agent building a full UI5 app from a natural language prompt. Jitendra Kansal's MDK session addressed how to bring Fiori to mobile without a rewrite, with the MDK MCP Server intelligently transforming UI for smaller screens. Mohammed Sijas introduced the DataRepository library, a lightweight solution that brings OData-like CRUD convenience to plain REST APIs.

Sandeep R S and Vinay Hospete reinforced that ~90% of SAP's own SAPUI5 apps are built on Fiori elements, and that MCP-powered agentic AI is the natural next step. Syed's session on Fiori Applications Demystified gave backend developers and architects a grounding in MVC, bindings, and when to choose Freestyle over Fiori Elements.

"Good place to know what's new and what is the future scope. Hands-on sessions give good insight on the topics."
Participant, Cargill

Accessibility, Web Components, and More

Gergana Georgieva's session on UI5's Ever-Evolving Accessibility was a highlight for many attendees. She walked through SAP's commitment to WCAG 2.2 and ARIA 1.2 standards, covering everything from keyboard navigation and screen reader support to the new Target Size requirements. The key message: accessibility requires both framework support and developer discipline — it must become a habit, not an afterthought.

"I really liked the accessibility session."
Participant, Wipro

The demo pod on UI5 Web Components — presented by Rounak, Vignesh, and Dharshini — showcased the growing maturity of the framework-agnostic component library built on web standards. Web Components 2.0 was announced, with new components including the intriguing <ui5-ai-button>. For developers working with React, Angular, or Vue, this opens new doors.

"It was very interactive. Now they are releasing web components 2.0 which looks promising."
Participant, Adidas

The Suite Controls session from Ashwin Kumar Bolar, Vinay K N, and Santhosh covered enterprise-grade components for complex business applications: Network Graph, UploadSetWithTable, and the Gantt Chart — each purpose-built for the kind of rich operational dashboards that SAP customers depend on. Kotresh T M rounded out the day with a deep dive into cross-app navigation for Fiori microfrontends, making the case for intent-based navigation as the standard pattern for loosely coupled Fiori apps.

 And for those excited about the future of AI in SAP applications, Arun Bharathi K's demo on Joule Frontend Actions showed how UI5 apps can expose capabilities directly to SAP's digital AI assistant — making Joule a conversational interface for everyday Fiori interactions.

"Got to know about the capability of Joule with the frontend actions. Would like to have a blog on this."
Participant, AMGBT

Hands-On Sessions: Learning by Doing

A major draw of UI5con has always been its hands-on sessions — and 2026 was no exception. Attendees rolled up their sleeves and built real UI5 apps, working through exercises on SAP Fiori elements V4, the Flexible Programming Model, and the UI5 Linter, with GitHub repositories shared so participants could take the work home. Volunteers circulated the room throughout, ensuring no one got stuck, and the atmosphere — equal parts focused and buzzing — was one of the highlights of the day.

“Attended 2 hands-on sessions of UI5con. Volunteers are always ready to help, amazing knowledgeable speakers. Loved the UI5con event.”
Participant, Deloitte

Ask the Expert: The Closing Highlight

The day closed with one of its most anticipated moments: the Ask the Expert panel. Peter Muessig, Vinod S Nair, Stefan Beck and Sandeep R S — some of the most prominent names behind UI5 and SAP Fiori — took to the stage together for an open, free-flowing conversation with the audience. The session was part Q&A, part fireside chat, and completely candid. Attendees asked about the roadmap, the future of legacy migrations, how best to adopt agentic AI in their own teams, and much more. It was exactly the kind of direct access to the people building the platform that makes UI5con special — and a fitting send-off for the day.

The Community Makes the Event

Across the feedback forms, one theme stood out as clearly as the technical content: people valued the community. Volunteers were praised for their patience, speakers for making complex topics accessible, and the hands-on format — with GitHub links, live demos, and side-by-side coding — gave participants something concrete to take home.

Looking Ahead

UI5con Bengaluru 2026 made one thing clear: seven years was too long to wait. The SAP UI5 ecosystem is moving faster than ever, the community is hungry for it, and Devanahalli proved it can host a world-class developer event. Thank you to every speaker, volunteer, and attendee who made this happen. See you at the next one.

"Looking forward for the next iteration of UI5con."
Participant, Volvo

entry.title: How to Migrate UI5 Projects to the Latest UI5 Best Practices
entry.content:
In September 2025, the initial release of UI5 MCP server was published. In Give Your AI Agent Some Tools: Introducing the UI5 MCP Server by @merlin_beutlberger you’ll find a great overview of the available features and a detailed step-by-step guide. This blog post focuses on how UI5 MCP server can support you in migrating your UI5 project easily to the latest best practices with the help of GitHub Copilot Coding Agent. The results are really good. 

Prerequisites: 

  • The repository that you'd like to migrate must be hosted on GitHub. 
  • Subscribe to a GitHub Copilot Pro license to use the GitHub Coding Agent feature. 

Initial Setup 

To use UI5 MCP server in GitHub Copilot Coding Agent, adjust the Coding Agent settings in your repository. Open your repository settings, select the Copilot item, and then select the Coding Agent.  

On the right side, there's an empty input area where you enter your MCP configuration. Use a similar configuration to the one you already use in your local VSCode settings. 

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "ui5-mcp-server": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "npx",
      "tools": [
        "run_ui5_linter",
        "get_guidelines",
        "get_project_info",
        "get_version_info",
        "get_api_reference",
        "get_typescript_conversion_guidelines"
      ],
      "args": [
        "@ui5/mcp-server"
      ]
    }
  }
}

Important: Don’t forget to press the Save MCP configuration button. 

Let’s start 

Now your repository is ready. Create a new issue on your GitHub repository (see flovogt/openui5-sample-app #165) with the title "Migrate to latest best practices". You could also be more specific by writing "Migrate to latest UI5 best practices", but if you want to modernize the whole project, choose the more general variant. 

After the issue is created, assign the GitHub Copilot user to the issue. Immediately, the Copilot user starts to analyze the issue and creates a draft PR flovogt/openui5-sample-app #166 to resolve the issue.

In the beginning, the PR description contains just a short recap of the issue to be fixed by this PR. However, in the background, the Copilot Coding Agent starts to create a plan to solve the issue. After a few more minutes (approximately two minutes), the PR description is updated with a detailed plan of what the Coding Agent does. UI5 MCP server plays a crucial role here. It uses UI5 linter and dedicated migration guides to make your project compliant with the latest best practices. 

After the plan is made, the Coding Agent goes into execution. It commits the results to the corresponding branch and marks the steps as completed in the PR description. 

After the job is done, the Coding Agent executes the tests to verify functional correctness. Having high code coverage is key here. Finally, the Coding Agent starts the project, creates a screenshot, and adds it to the PR description, plus summarizing the impact of all the steps. 

The overall migration took only a few minutes, and the code quality is really good - at least when I tried it. Don’t blame me if you experience bad code - we're in the world of AI after all 😊 GitHub Copilot Coding Agent and UI5 MCP server work really well together. 

 

JavaScript to TypeScript Migration 

Enough of praising the AI. Let’s challenge Copilot and UI5 MCP server even further. You might have already read the blog post UI5 MCP Server's New TypeScript Migration Feature - Real-World Experience and Best Practices by @AndreasKunz. In this case, you're already aware that UI5 MCP server supports such a migration feature. But the overall experience, without checking out any code locally, is just amazing. I created another issue flovogt/openui5-sample-app #167 to ask GitHub Copilot to migrate the whole legacy project to TypeScript and let the Coding Agent do the work in PR flovogt/openui5-sample-app #168.  

And the result is awesome! The whole legacy project was migrated to TypeScript in less than half an hour.  

 

Summary

I was really surprised by how well the overall integration of UI5 MCP server works. The whole job would have taken me at least one day to do it right. The agent does most of the work, and I can focus on code review and other tasks still to be done by humans. Welcome to the world of AI, UI5!

entry.title: Building AI Skills for the Future of the SAP Ecosystem
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence in the SAP ecosystem is entering a new chapter. We’ve moved from analytics to predictions, from predictions to generative AI, and now to Agentic AI; and this is where it gets truly exciting.

What Is Agentic AI?

Agentic AI goes beyond generating text or answering prompts. It can pursue goals, plan, decide, interact with systems, and execute multi-step actions within defined guardrails. While generative AI creates content, agentic AI drives outcomes, which is what matters most in enterprise environments.

In SAP, where processes connect finance, supply chain, procurement, and HR, this shift has enormous potential. SAP landscapes are structured, data-rich, and process-driven, ideal for intelligent agents that actively support and orchestrate business execution.

Imagine AI not only spotting a supply chain risk but triggering alternative sourcing workflows or detecting anomalies in financial postings and initiating clarification tasks automatically. This isn’t science fiction, it’s the direction enterprise AI is heading: from assistance to collaboration.

Under SAP’s Business AI strategy, AI is becoming embedded directly into applications and workflows. The vision is clear: AI as a digital co-worker. Not replacing people but augmenting them. Not adding complexity but reducing friction. Agentic AI is the next evolution in making that vision real.

 

 

Want to dive deeper into Agentic AI? Discover the mini agent blog series by @shannabauer and @christian_karaschewitz:

  • Envision the Future of Generative AI with SAP's AI Agents
  • AI Agents in Action
  • AI Agents - Thinking Fast & Thinking Slow, Balancing Autonomy and Determinism in Joule agents

 

Learning and Hands-On Opportunities for All Things AI

If you’re curious about how to identify real business impact, the course “Discovering High-Value Opportunities for Agentic AI” helps bridge innovation and value creation; a crucial skill as AI initiatives become more strategic. For a practical, hands-on introduction to working with intelligent agents, the Devtoberfest 2025 session “Getting Started with Agents using SAP Generative AI Hub” walks through the basics of building and experimenting with agent scenarios. Explore the full AI playlist for more resources with SAP Tech Bytes, Ask the Expert sessions and more on the SAP Developers YouTube channel. And for even more AI news, check out the monthly blog series that spotlights the latest AI learning assets and upcoming learning opportunities.

And the learning journey doesn’t stop there. SAP also offers several upcoming free live sessions open to anyone to bring AI innovation directly into practical enterprise scenarios:

  • Efficient Document Inbound Processing in SAP Cloud ERP with SAP Document AI – March 12, 2026: Discover how SAP Document AI streamlines inbound document handling in SAP Cloud ERP, reducing manual effort and increasing automation in finance and procurement processes.
  • All Things Business AI: Latest Insights, Updates & Live Demos – March 19, 2026: Get first-hand insights into SAP’s Business AI strategy, explore the newest updates, and experience live demonstrations of AI capabilities embedded across the SAP portfolio.
  • Build AI Securely – Avoid Pitfalls in the Development and Operations Lifecycle – April 9, 2026: Learn how to design, develop, and operate AI solutions securely, while avoiding common risks in governance, compliance, and lifecycle management.

 

Ready to dive deeper? Don’t miss the upcoming local SAP CodeJam events, where you can get hands-on experience with Generative AI Hub, SAP Joule, and AI-powered applications:

  • March 16, 2026:  Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Risch-Rotkreuz, Switzerland)
  • March 17, 2026:  Joule Studio: Create Joule Skills and Agents Without Coding (Risch-Rotkreuz, Switzerland)
  • March 20, 2026: Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Madrid, Spain)
  • March 20, 2026:  Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Belo Horizonte, Brazil)
  • April 7, 2026: ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (São Paulo, Brazil)
  • April 8, 2026:  ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (Londrina, Brazil)
  • April 10, 2026: Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Curitiba, Brazil)
  • April 10, 2026: SAP HANA Cloud for AI Applications: ML, Vectors, and Knowledge Graphs (Ribeirão Preto, Brazil)
  • And much more – check out the full list of all upcoming events here.

 

AI Literacy: Core Competency for SAP Professionals

Here’s the most important part: you don’t need to be a data scientist to participate in this transformation. What you do need is AI literacy. Understanding the fundamentals of artificial intelligence, generative AI, and how SAP AI services integrate into enterprise processes is quickly becoming a core competency for SAP professionals.

For developers ready to build AI-powered applications, the SAP Generative AI Developer certification validates hands-on expertise in designing and integrating generative AI within SAP environments. For consultants and presales professionals shaping customer conversations, the Positioning SAP Business AI Solutions as Part of the SAP Business Suite certification strengthens the ability to translate AI capabilities into business value.

Agentic AI represents a shift from “What can AI tell me?” to “What can AI achieve with me?”

For those working in the SAP ecosystem, this is more than a trend — it’s the next capability layer on top of digital transformation. The professionals who understand how to design, position, and work alongside AI agents will shape the next generation of enterprise processes.

The future of SAP is intelligent. The future of AI is agentic. And the opportunity to grow with it starts now.

entry.title: Scaling Generative AI in SAP S/4HANA: Enterprise-Ready Innovation with ISLM
entry.content:
• Introduction and context
  • Key Enhancements for Generative AI Enablement in ISLM
  • Availability and Downport Strategy Across SAP S/4HANA Releases
  • Deep Dive: Latest ISLM Capabilities for Generative AI
  • Orchestration Service Integration
  • Developing Custom Generative AI Use Cases — Step-by-Step guide
  • Support for new models offered by Generative AI Hub
  • Developing Your Own AI-Enabled Applications in SAP ABAP Cloud with ISLM and ABAP AI SDK
  • Conclusion

Introduction and context

In today’s rapidly evolving digital landscape, Generative AI  is transforming how enterprises innovate, automate, and deliver business value . To successfully embed Generative AI within SAP S/4HANA, organizations need more than access to large language models. They require a structured, scalable, and governed framework to integrate, operate, and manage AI use cases across their landscape.

Over the past year, these Generative AI capabilities are now enabled through ISLM, helping customers and partners move from experimentation to productive usage. You can learn more through my previous blog post and the  YouTube webinar replay which outline the architecture, supported use cases, and how ISLM integrates Generative AI into SAP S/4HANA SAP business applications. 

Building on this foundation, this blog focuses on the latest and upcoming enhancements in Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management that further strengthen Generative AI enablement. We will explore what’s new, how these enhancements simplify GenAI integration and lifecycle management, and how customers and partners can leverage them to accelerate innovation and scale AI-driven business scenarios in SAP S/4HANA.

Key Enhancements for Generative AI Enablement in ISLM

  1. Integration with the Orchestration Service:  ISLM now integrates with the Orchestration Service to deliver advanced, enterprise-grade  capabilities for Generative AI scenarios. This includes Document Grounding to enrich prompts with business-relevant context, Content Filtering to ensure safe and compliant responses, and Data Masking to protect sensitive information before it is processed by large language models. In addition, the service supports input and output translation for multilingual use cases and incorporates a Prompt Shield filter to help defend against prompt injection attacks. Together, these capabilities significantly enhance the security, reliability, and governance of Generative AI embedded within SAP business applications.
  2. Support for new models offered by Generative AI Hub: ISLM enables customers to leverage state-of-the-art large language models while maintaining flexibility and future readiness.
  3. Reuse Connectivity to Gen AI Hub:  A shared connectivity model allows a single connection between the ABAP system and the SAP Generative AI Hub to be configured once and reused across multiple AI use cases. This simplifies administration, improves governance, and reduces setup effort.
  4. Simplification of creation of Intelligent Scenario: Creation of Intelligent Scenarios has been simplified, moving from a pro-code approach to a more streamlined low-code experience.In addition, ISLM allows the optional use of the prediction class for Generative AI scenarios. 

Availability and Downport Strategy Across SAP S/4HANA Releases 

To accelerate the adoption of Generative AI, the latest ISLM enhancements have been downported to lower SAP S/4HANA releases, enabling customers to benefit from new capabilities without waiting for a full system upgrade. These enhancements are available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise.

Supported Releases (No TCI Required)
For the following Support Package (SP) levels, the latest capabilities are already included — no additional TCI implementation is required:

  • SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01
  • SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS06

Lower SP Levels (TCI Required)
For lower SP levels within the 2025 and 2023 releases, the relevant Transport-based Correction Instructions (TCIs) must be implemented to enable the latest ISLM capabilities.

By delivering these enhancements via TCIs, customers can adopt the newest ISLM innovations without requiring a complete Support Package update. This approach significantly reduces upgrade complexity while providing faster access to the latest Generative AI functionality in ISLM.

To enable the new capabilities, please implement the corresponding SAP Notes listed below.

  • SAP Note 3682148
  • SAP Note 3668194

Deep Dive: Latest ISLM Capabilities for Generative AI

Orchestration Service Integration

ISLM supports integration with the Orchestration Service on SAP AI Core, enabling advanced Generative AI capabilities such as document grounding, content filtering, and data masking. Additional features like input and output translation and a Prompt Shield filter help protect against prompt injection attacks, strengthening security. New API enhancements further improve grounding quality through larger chunk sizes and application-specific document chunking. Together, these capabilities enable standardized, flexible, and enterprise-ready GenAI integration—allowing customers to switch between foundation models without code changes and reinforcing ISLM as the central framework for governing and scaling Generative AI across SAP landscapes.

The following modules within the orchestration service are supported by ISLM :

  1. Data Masking – Protects sensitive information by masking or removing confidential data.
  2. Input Filtering – Validates and sanitizes incoming data before it is processed by the LLM.
  3. Output Filtering – Reviews and filters the LLM-generated output to ensure compliance and relevance.
  4. Grounding – Aligns model outputs with enterprise data or domain-specific knowledge to improve response accuracy.
Developing Custom Generative AI Use Cases — Step-by-Step guide

Following information is relevant for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition & On-Premise releases 
For step-by-step guide on using orchestration within ISLM, let’s consider the following example use case:

Use Case: AI-Driven Return Policy Analysis with ISLM

In this scenario, two fictional sports brands — Company Alpha and Company Beta — maintain different return policies with varying eligibility rules, timelines, refund conditions, and exceptions.

Customer support teams frequently handle queries such as:

  • “Is this item still eligible for return?”
  • “Are customized products refundable?”
  • “How long will the refund take?”

Manually reviewing policy documents is time-consuming and can lead to inconsistent responses.
Using ISLM with orchestration: 

  • Policy documents are uploaded for document grounding.
  • User queries are processed through the orchestration workflow.
  • The LLM extracts relevant clauses and generates structured responses.

Business Value

  • Reduces manual effort
  • Speeds up response time
  • Ensures consistent policy interpretation
  • Enables automated decision support within business processes

Implementation Steps 

  1. Set and configure SAP AI Core
  2. Design and development of the Orchestration Workflow
  3. Export workflow configuration
  4. Create and deploy Intelligent Scenarios 
  5. Consumption of the Intelligent Scenario with ABAP AI SDK APIs

Prerequisites for using Gen AI scenario in ISLM. Ensure that you have completed the following steps before continuing with creating your own Gen AI scenario: Prerequisites | SAP Help Portal

1) Access AI Launchpad: Design and Development of the Orchestration Workflow
Open an available instance of the SAP Generative AI Hub – >AI Launchpad to start prompt engineering and configure your orchestration workflow.

1.1) Configure Orchestration Modules
Within the AI Launchpad, configure the required modules for the orchestration service according to your use case, refer to the following link:  Build Your Orchestration Workflow | SAP Help Portal

Sample Orchestration Configuration 

{
	"modules": {
		"grounding": {
			"type": "document_grounding_service",
			"config": {
				"filters": [],
				"placeholders": {
					"input": [
						"grounding_input_variable_1"
					],
					"output": "grounding_output_variable"
				}
			}
		},
		"prompt_templating": {
			"prompt": {
				"template": [
					{
						"role": "user",
						"content": [
							{
								"type": "text",
								"text": "{{?grounding_output_variable}}"
							}
						]
					}
				],
				"defaults": {}
			},
			"model": {
				"name": "gpt-5-mini",
				"params": {
					"max_tokens": 64000,
					"temperature": 1
				},
				"version": "1"
			}
		},
		"filtering": {
			"input": {
				"filters": [
					{
						"type": "azure_content_safety",
						"config": {
							"hate": 2,
							"self_harm": 2,
							"sexual": 2,
							"violence": 2
						}
					},
					{
						"type": "llama_guard_3_8b",
						"config": {
							"child_exploitation": true,
							"code_interpreter_abuse": true,
							"defamation": true,
							"elections": true,
							"hate": true,
							"indiscriminate_weapons": true,
							"intellectual_property": true,
							"non_violent_crimes": true,
							"privacy": true,
							"self_harm": true,
							"sex_crimes": true,
							"sexual_content": true,
							"specialized_advice": true,
							"violent_crimes": true
						}
					}
				]
			},
			"output": {
				"filters": [
					{
						"type": "azure_content_safety",
						"config": {
							"hate": 2,
							"self_harm": 2,
							"sexual": 2,
							"violence": 2
						}
					},
					{
						"type": "llama_guard_3_8b",
						"config": {
							"child_exploitation": true,
							"code_interpreter_abuse": true,
							"defamation": true,
							"elections": true,
							"hate": true,
							"indiscriminate_weapons": true,
							"intellectual_property": true,
							"non_violent_crimes": true,
							"privacy": true,
							"self_harm": true,
							"sex_crimes": true,
							"sexual_content": true,
							"specialized_advice": true,
							"violent_crimes": true
						}
					}
				]
			}
		},
		"masking": {
			"masking_providers": [
				{
					"type": "sap_data_privacy_integration",
					"method": "anonymization",
					"entities": [
						{
							"type": "profile-credit-card-number"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-driverlicense"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-email"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-sensitive-data"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-ethnicity"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-gender"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-pronouns-gender"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-iban"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-location"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-nationalid"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-nationality"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-org"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-passport"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-person"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-phone"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-political-group"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-sapids-public"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-religious-group"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-sapids-internal"

						{

						{

						{

						{

						{

						{

				"type": "sap_document_translation",
				"config": {
					"source_language": "",
					"target_language": "en-US"
				}
			},
			"output": {
				"type": "sap_document_translation",
				"config": {
					"source_language": "",
					"target_language": "USER_LANG"

}

It is mandatory to have a valid orchestration configuration as a workflow. Ensure the orchestration workflow is configured and exported as a JSON file. Refer to the above section.

1.2) Export Workflow Configuration
After all necessary modules have been configured, export the orchestration workflow configuration as a JSON file for use or deployment.

2) Reuse Connectivity

A shared connectivity model allows a single connection between the ABAP system and the SAP Generative AI Hub to be configured once and reused across multiple AI use cases. This simplifies administration, improves governance, and reduces setup effort.

Maintain Intelligent Scenario Usage Type Connections | SAP Help Portal

 3) Create the Intelligent Scenario

3.1) Launch the SAP Fiori Launchpad and open the Intelligent Scenarios app.

Choose Create → Side-by-Side, then in the Settings tab:

  • Enter name and description
  • Select Generative AI as the Scenario Type
  • ISLM allows optional use of the prediction class for Generative AI scenarios 
  • Select Stateless – Customer as the Usage Type, which points to the reuse connectivity created in the previous step.

SAP Help Portal | SAP Online Help

 3.2)  Add Generative AI Model to the Intelligent scenario

Choose Add Model and maintain the required details:

  • Enter a model name and description
  • Select the appropriate Executable ID and corresponding model
  • (Optional) Choose a specific model version (latest is selected by default)
  • Optionally add a prompt template
  • Choose Add to confirm

3.3) Intelligent scenario with Orchestration service

    • Upload  the orchestration Execution Flow JSON in this model to integrate the orchestration service.
    • Use the Upload button to add the execution flow template or Copy the JSON into the dialog.  

  • Navigate to the Execution Flow Template
    • Once a valid JSON is uploaded, the modules will be displayed with their parameters and values in display mode.

In the Grounding subsection, add the required data repository types (vector, help.sap.com)

To restrict your grounding module output during inference, maintain Search Configuration value. This can help to retrieve only relevant data instead of all the uploaded data.

The Input Translation module allows you to translate LLM text prompts and grounding module output into a target language. It may help improve LLM response when the configured model performs better with input in specific language, example English.

The Data Masking module enables the anonymization or pseudonymization of data before it is sent to the LLM model for processing.

The input filters defined in the Execution Flow Template JSON are populated and displayed in the table.

The Output Filtering module allows you to filter the harmful or hateful content generated by the LLM. 

The Output Translation module allows you to translate LLM response into a target language. It helps to display the LLM response in the language user logged, if the translation is supported.

3.4) Add Prompt Template

 

 Add the grounding template

Add a Grounding Template. The grounding template can include dynamic parameters similar to the user prompt.

Add Prompt Template | SAP Help Portal

The Grounding Template retrieves relevant information from the selected data repository and appends it to the user prompt. It supports dynamic parameters, which are provided at runtime (during inference).

Add user prompt

If you have configured grounding module, at least one User Prompt must be added with the following parameter: {ISLM_GROUNDING_OUTPUT}

At runtime, this placeholder is automatically replaced with the content retrieved by the Grounding Template from the configured data repository.

add-prompt-template| SAP Help Portal 

3.6) Upload grounding documents and publish Intelligent scenario

 

Upload documents to an Intelligent Scenario, refer to the following link: SAP Help Portal | Upload Grounding Document

Below are sample return policy documents for Company Alpha and Company Beta that you can use for this example use case.

You can copy the content into separate .txt files (for example: company_alpha_return_policy.txt and company_beta_return_policy.txt) and upload them to your orchestration workflow for document grounding.

Company Alpha Return Policy

**Eligibility:**
- Items must be purchased from Alpha.com, Alpha App, or Alpha stores.
- Items must be in original condition, unworn and unwashed.
- Alpha By You custom products are eligible for return within the return window.

**Time Frame:**
- Standard items: 60 days from purchase date.
- Holiday purchases may have extended return windows.

**Condition of Items:**
- Items must include original packaging and accessories.
- Apparel must have tags attached.

**Refund Methods:**
- Refunds issued to original payment method.
- PayPal in-store purchases refunded via Alpha gift card.
- Refunds typically processed within 10 days of receipt.

**Exceptions:**
- Items from Alpha Clearance stores are not eligible.
- Items purchased from other retailers cannot be returned to Alpha.

**Return Process:**
1. Initiate return via Alpha account order history.
2. Print prepaid UPS label or use QR code.
3. Drop off at UPS or schedule pickup.
4. Refund issued after inspection.

   -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Company Beta Return Policy

**Eligibility:**
- Items must be purchased from us.Beta.com or Beta retail stores.
- Custom products are nonrefundable unless defective.
- Gift cards and bodywear including face masks are not eligible for return.

**Time Frame:**
- Standard items: 45 days from the date of purchase.
- MB.01/MB.02 Basketball Shoes: 15 days.


**Condition of Items:**
- Items must be unused and in original packaging.
- Include original form of payment and order confirmation for store returns.

**Refund Methods:**
- Refunds are issued to the original payment method.
- Beta Gift Card refunds are issued as separate E-Gift Cards.
- Refunds may take 1–2 weeks to process.

**Exceptions:**
- No returns on gift cards, customized products, and bodywear.
- No free returns for APO/FPO addresses and US Territories.

**Return Process:**
1. Initiate return via Beta returns portal.
2. Use pre-paid shipping label provided.
3. Drop off at designated location.
4. Refund processed upon receipt and inspection.

4)Deploy & Activate

Once the Intelligent Scenario is created, we can perform operations such as deployment and activation using Intelligent Scenario Management App.

Click Deploy.

You can now activate the deployment to consume the inference in your business application. You can activate deployment for your user or all users by selecting the For Me and For All options respectively.

5)Consumption of Intelligent Scenario with ABAP AI SDK

Leverage the Intelligent Scenario, together with the defined orchestration workflow, to drive automated decision-making within your business processes.

The ABAP AI SDK provides dedicated APIs to seamlessly consume inference and prompt execution results directly within your ABAP application logic.

Prompt Execution with Orchestration Service:
Use the intelligent scenario within the orchestration to drive decision-making and automation.  Once the selected deployment is activated, you can use ABAP AI SDK for inference consumption. For reference, see Utilization Of The Orchestration Service Of The Generative AI Hub.

Sample Code

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report Z_TEST_ISLM_REPORT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
REPORT Z_TEST_ISLM_REPORT.

PARAMETERS: company TYPE string OBLIGATORY.

CONSTANTS lv_scenario TYPE islm_de_sbs_is_name VALUE 'Z_ORCHESTRATION_SCEN'.

TRY.

    " Create an instance of the ABAP GenAI SDK based on an ISLM Scenario
    FINAL(api) = cl_aic_islm_orch_api_factory=>get( )->create_instance( lv_scenario ).

    api->configure_grounding( )->add_grounding_template( template_id = 'GROUNDING_TEMPLATE'
                                                         template_parameters = VALUE #( ( name = 'ISLM_GROUNDING_QUERY'
                                                                                          value = company ) ) ).

    api->configure_templating( )->add_prompt_template( message_type = aic_prompt_message_type=>user_message
                                                       template_id  = 'SUMMARIZE_RETURN_POLICY' ).

    api->set_input_parameter( name  = 'ISLM_COMPANY_NAME'
                              value = company ).

    " execute
    FINAL(response) = api->execute( ).

    " get the completion
    FINAL(answer) = response->orchestration_result( )->completion( ).

    DATA(lv_status) = 'S'.

    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = answer ).

  CATCH cx_aic_api_factory INTO DATA(lx_factory).
    DATA(error) = lx_factory->get_text( ).
    lv_status = 'E'.
    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = error ).
  CATCH cx_aic_completion_api INTO DATA(lx_completion).
    DATA(error2) = lx_completion->get_text( ).
    lv_status = 'E'.
    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = error2 ).
  CATCH cx_aic_prompt_template INTO DATA(lx_prompt_template).
    DATA(error3) = lx_prompt_template->get_text( ).
    lv_status = 'E'.
    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = error3 ).
  CATCH cx_aic_orchestration_api INTO DATA(lx_orchestration).
    DATA(error4) = lx_orchestration->get_text( ).
    lv_status = 'E'.
    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = error4 ).
ENDTRY.

Run the sample report with Company = COMPANY AlPHA

The output is a summary generated by large language models (LLMs).

Support for new models offered by Generative AI Hub

With the ABAP AI SDK, powered by ISLM, support has been extended to large language models (LLMs), including the following:

gpt-5
gpt-5-mini
gpt-5-nano
o3
o4-mini
gemini-2.5-flash
gemini-2.5-pro
amazon--nova-pro
amazon--nova-lite
amazon--nova-micro
mistralai--mistral-medium-instruct
mistralai--mistral-small-instruct

This enhancement broadens model support, enabling alignment with varied enterprise requirements. For detailed information on supported models, availability, please refer to the relevant SAP Note 3668194 .

Developing Your Own AI-Enabled Applications in SAP ABAP Cloud with ISLM and ABAP AI SDK 

By leveraging ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM, you can enrich your custom ABAP applications with seamlessly integrated Generative AI capabilities. This enables you to embed intelligent, context-aware features directly into your business processes while maintaining governance and clean core principles. This approach enables customers to extend SAP applications in a future-proof and upgrade-safe manner. 

Together, ISLM and ABAP AI SDK offer a powerful yet governed framework for embedding Generative AI into ABAP applications, ensuring enterprise-level reliability, security, and scalability. 

For detailed  guidance and  steps, refer to the SAP Help Portal | Developing Your Own AI-Enabled Applications: 

To get started, refer to the SAP Help Portal  .

Conclusion

Generative AI delivers real enterprise value when it is embedded directly into business processes with the right governance, scalability, and control. Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management  provides exactly that foundation, enabling structured creation, orchestration, deployment, and lifecycle management of AI scenarios within SAP S/4HANA.

With advanced runtime capabilities such as Document Grounding, Content Filtering, Data Masking, multilingual support,  reusable connectivity, and expanded model support, ISLM transforms Generative AI from experimentation into operational reality. Get started today and take your Generative AI innovation to the next level with ISLM!!

entry.title: Knowledge Graphs on SAP HANA: From Zero to Enterprise RAG with Triple Store
entry.content:
Introduction

If you’ve ever worked with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, you know the drill, you embed your documents into vectors, store them in a vector database, and at query time, you retrieve the top-K most similar chunks to feed as context to an LLM. It works. But there’s a problem.

Vector search alone is semantically “flat.” It tells you which chunks look similar to a query, but it doesn’t understand the relationships between entities in your data. It cannot reason about connections, follow chains of facts, or understand how entity A relates to entity B through entity C.

This is where Knowledge Graphs come in.

In this blog, I’ll walk you through — from absolute scratch — how i built a Knowledge Graph layer on SAP HANA using a custom Triple Store, and how i combined it with vector embedding-based retrieval to create a Hybrid RAG system that gives our AI agents both relevance (from vectors) and reasoning (from graphs).

Everything you see is from POC codebase. Let’s begin.

Table of Contents

  1. What is a Knowledge Graph?
  2. What are RDF Triplets?
  3. Why Do We Need Knowledge Graphs in RAG?
  4. Extracting Triplets from Text
    • 4a. Rule-Based Extraction with spaCy
    • 4b. LLM-Based Extraction
  5. The Multi-Agent Triplet Processing Pipeline
  6. Storing Triplets in SAP HANA Triple Store
  7. The Complete Ingestion Pipeline
  8. Querying the Knowledge Graph
  9. Hybrid Retrieval: Vector + Graph
  10. The Full RAG Pipeline
  11. Exposing via MCP for AI Agents
  12. Architecture Summary
  13. Conclusion

1. What is a Knowledge Graph?

A Knowledge Graph is a structured representation of real-world entities and the relationships between them. Think of it as a web of connected facts.

For example, consider this sentence:

“SAP SE is headquartered in Walldorf, Germany and develops enterprise software.”
